coredata.dc:title: ‘Autophagy’ and unique aerial oxygen harvesting properties exhibited by highly photocatalytic carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Sanjit
Vinod, C.P.
Gautam, Ujjal K.
coredata.dc:description: Plastic pollution is a serious threat to the environment as they are not bio-degradable. Herein using waste-polyethylene, we report two inter-related important findings. First that they can be converted economically and completely into highly photocatalytic carbon quantum-dots (CQDs) using H2O2 as a residue-free oxidant in an acid medium, and they have the ability to enrich a reaction medium with an extraordinary amount of molecular oxygen harvested from the air so that no separate oxygen source is needed while carrying out facile photocatalytic oxidation reactions. Second, when they are not performing any photocatalytic reaction, they exhibit self-sensitized photo-oxidation to convert themselves completely to CO2 in the presence of sunlight, a phenomenon that we term as ‘CQD-autophagy’ by drawing parallels to the death of useless-cells in the body. Such extinction from the reaction media is unique as no other material is known to do so and advantageous because the removal of carbon-dots from a solution is rather inefficient from an industry-viewpoint due to their high solubility. Instead, the inexpensive CQDs can be discarded casually with the notion that they will gradually get eliminated from the environment.

head.ce:title: ‘Autophagy’ and unique aerial oxygen harvesting properties exhibited by highly photocatalytic carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Sciences, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research (IISER)-Mohali, Sector 81, Mohali, SAS Nagar, Punjab, 140306, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Catalysis and Inorganic Chemistry Division, CSIR-NCL, Pune, 411008, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Catalysis and Inorganic Chemistry Division, CSIR-NCL, Pune, 411008, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plastic pollution is a serious threat to the environment as they are not bio-degradable. Herein using waste-polyethylene, we report two inter-related important findings. First that they can be converted economically and completely into highly photocatalytic carbon quantum-dots (CQDs) using H2O2 as a residue-free oxidant in an acid medium, and they have the ability to enrich a reaction medium with an extraordinary amount of molecular oxygen harvested from the air so that no separate oxygen source is needed while carrying out facile photocatalytic oxidation reactions. Second, when they are not performing any photocatalytic reaction, they exhibit self-sensitized photo-oxidation to convert themselves completely to CO2 in the presence of sunlight, a phenomenon that we term as ‘CQD-autophagy’ by drawing parallels to the death of useless-cells in the body. Such extinction from the reaction media is unique as no other material is known to do so and advantageous because the removal of carbon-dots from a solution is rather inefficient from an industry-viewpoint due to their high solubility. Instead, the inexpensive CQDs can be discarded casually with the notion that they will gradually get eliminated from the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Waste-plastic poses a serious threat to the environment due to their non-biodegradable nature [1–5]. Therefore, the reduction of plastic-waste has emerged as a research priority, leading to many innovative approaches to convert them into useful materials such as liquid-fuels and related materials [6–8]. Recently, some of us demonstrated that waste-polyethylene (PE) can be used as precursors for obtaining large quantities of visible light responsive carbon quantum-dots (CQDs) by using H2SO4 as a dehydrogenating agent [9]. The CQDs are a distinctive class of carbon-nanomaterials containing tiny graphene-sheets with many potential applications such as in photocatalysis, optoelectronics, imaging, diagnostics etc. [10–15] PE-derived CQDs (PE-CQDs) exhibited a unique property while dispersed in a solution that they are capable of adsorbing exorbitantly large quantity of molecular O2 (∼1 wt%) from the air onto its surface under ambient conditions. Coupled this to their ability to absorb solar-light has resulted in high photocatalytic efficiency for photo-oxidation reactions for fine chemical synthesis and waste-water treatment, while in contrast, the CQDs are usually utilized for photo-reduction reactions [16–18].

However, the use of PE-CQDs, or CQDs in general as photocatalysts has two major concerns: (i) the recovery of the used CQDs from a reaction medium is difficult due to high solubility and may eventually find their way to natural water bodies, when used in large quantities in the industry. Similar difficulties may be envisioned for many other efficient photocatalysts as well, particularly when used in large-scale. (ii) The synthesis of PE-CQDs requires toxic KMnO4 for oxidation, while all other chemicals used in the process are potentially reusable. Besides being toxic, MnO2 generated in the process remains in the H2SO4 solution and hence this solution cannot be repeatedly used for subsequent batches of synthesis, raising concern about sustainability of the synthesis procedure. Therefore, it is necessary to eliminate the use of KMnO4 is the synthesis PE-CQDs and, at the same time, to analyze the impact of the used PE-CQDs left unattended in an open environment.

Herein, we demonstrate an efficient route to produce the PE-CQDs from polyethylene using H2O2, instead of KMnO4, as a residue-free, non-toxic oxidant. These PE-CQDs exhibited an improved oxygen-harvesting ability from the air and enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for the oxidative transformation of organic molecules. We also show that when the PE-CQDs are exposed to sunlight for several weeks in the absence of reactants, they disappear completely from the solution, leaving no traces. Our experiments confirm that this phenomenon occurs due to a self-sensitized photo-oxidation process by using the molecular oxygen that they harvest from the air, to form carbon dioxide. This ‘self-decaying’ or ‘self-devouring’ nature that we refer to as ‘CQD-autophagy’, drawing a parallel to the self-decaying nature of the useless cells in our body, appears to be unique to the PE-CQDs as no other material and photocatalyst is known to undergo a similar transformation. In this context, it may be pointed out that ‘bleaching’ or color-fading of carbon-dot solutions have been widely observed and researched leading to several hypotheses [19–22]. The findings here are an important benchmark that ‘CQD-bleaching’ is part of ‘CQD-autophagy’ and any underlying bleaching mechanism ought to be consistent with an eventual CO2 formation.

We have demonstrated the high photocatalytic efficiency of the PE-CQDs from the controlled oxidation of benzyl alcohol (BA) using the air-harvested molecular O2 and ambient conditions. Semiconducting-material-driven photocatalytic reactions are emerging as a preferred process to utilize solar energy as a clean and renewable fuel [23–27]. The oxidation of BA and its derivatives is an important chemical transformation where the reaction products are widely used in fine-chemical synthesis [28–31]. Therein, the use of molecular O2 as the oxidant from a flowing gas, or even better, ambient air, is preferred due to its green nature and abundance. Hence it occurs that the O2 enriched PE-CQDs ought to be an outstanding photocatalyst for this selective oxidation reaction. The PE-CQDs induced ∼75% conversion of benzylalcohol and its derivatives to the corresponding benzaldehydes with >90% selectivity under 4 h of natural sunlight exposure. Besides, the unique ‘CQD-autophagy’ is expected to be highly valuable to remove them from their site-of-use where their recovery is economically unfeasible.

Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98%, Merck), Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, Merck), Sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, Rankem, 99%), ethyl acetate (Rankem, 99%), Acetonitrile (CH3CN, AR, 99.5%), Benzylalcohol (99%, Sigma), 4-chlorobenzylalcohol (C7H8NCl, Alfa Aesar, >97%), 4-fluorobenzylalcohol (C7H8FN, Alfa Aesar, >98%), Ammonium oxalate (C2H8N2O4, Sigma Aldrich, >99%), Potassium persulfate (K2S2O8, Sigma Aldrich, 99%), (Benzoquinone (C6H4O2, Alfa Aesar, 98%), ((2,2,6,6 –Tetramethylpiperidine-1-yl)oxyl)) (TEMPO, Sigma-Aldrich, 98%) were used without further purification. Milli-Q water was used for all the experiments. Polyethylene sheets (HDPE) were collected from IISER-Mohali campus.

The PE-CQDs were prepared by using H2O2 as oxidizing reagent after the carbonization of polyethylene. In a typical synthesis, 1 g of small pieces of polyethylene sheets were added to 60 mL of 18 M sulphuric acid in a 250 mL round bottom flask. The solution was refluxed at ∼140–150 °C with constant stirring at 600 rpm for 1 h. The PE sheets turned into black charred-mass. Thereafter, 1–2 mL of H2O2 was slowly added to the reaction mixture drop-wise resulting in a red color solution. The reaction mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. The solution was then diluted by adding 60 mL of water and then filtered by using cotton on the top of glass funnel. The transparent red colored solution was then extracted repeatedly by adding ethyl acetate using a separating funnel. The organic phase was then passed through the Na2SO4 to remove moisture and dried using a rota-vapour under reduced pressure, yielding solid powder of the graphitic PE-CQDs.

The PE-CQDs were characterized by using powder XRD (X-ray Diffraction, Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation, 10–80⁰ range), UV–Vis Spectroscopy (Agilent Cary 60-UV-Vis Spectrometer), PL (Photoluminescence Spectroscopy, Horiba Scientific), Time correlated single photon counts, (TCSPC, Fluorocube, Horiba JobinYvon, NJ), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscope (FT-IR, PerkinElmer), XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Thermo Kalpha + spectrometer using monochromated Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV) and micro focused. Quantum yield (QY) of the PE-CQDs was calculated by taking quinine sulphate as the standard (QY of 54%) in 0.1 M H2SO4. The high-resolution C 1s XPS peak was fitted to five Gaussian-Lorentzian peaks by constraining the equal Gaussian-Lorentzian pre-factors, while leaving the height of all peaks to be freely variable. The O 1s spectrum was also fitted similarly. Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-F200) was used to investigate the morphology and size of the PE-CQDs. TEM specimens were prepared by dispersing the PE-CQDs in ethanol and adding onto a porous holey carbon-coated copper grid. TCSPC data were collected by using laser diode head of 375 nm (NanoLED, Horiba Scientific) as an excitation source and all decay traces are recorded at different emission wavelength (400–600 nm range). The instrument response function (IRF) was collected by using colloidal solution of silica particles (Ludox, Sigma Aldrich) at 375 nm and found to be 310 ps. All data were collected by fixing peak preset at 10,000 counts and keeping the polarization at the magic angle (54.50). DAS6 (Horiba Scientific) software was used to analyze the lifetime (maintaining best-fitting parameters). An aqueous dispersion of PE-CQD (2.5 mg/5 mL) was used to record the PL spectra and TCSPC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxygen content measurements in the aqueous solution of PE-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: O2 contents in pure water and aqueous solution of PE-CQDs were measured using non-invasive Ocean Optics Neofox-Kit-Probe [9,32]. 50 mg of PE-CQDs dispersed in 60 mL of water was placed in a round bottom flask (RBF, capacity ∼70 mL) where the air-water interface area was 7 cm2. The sensing patch, attached to the inner wall of RBF, was completely submerged by the solution. During O2 uptake, the solution was stirred at 300 rpm and during O2 desorption the solution was kept still with a constant N2 flow (60–70 sccm). The kit was calibrated before all measurements using a two-point reading (0% O2 in N2 and 20.90% O2 in the air).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic benzyl alcohol oxidation using PE-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selective photo-oxidation of benzyl alcohol (BA) was carried out under direct sunlight as well as using a 400 W Xenon lamp (Newport) attached with an water-IR filter. Typically, 10 mg of PE-CQDs and 0.5 mmol of BA were dissolved in 10 mL of acetonitrile in a 10 mL RBF. Before the photocatalytic reaction, the reaction mixture was kept in dark for 1 h under constant magnetic stirring to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The temperature during the photocatalytic reaction was monitored and found to be ∼28–30 °C throughout the reaction period. After 4 h light illumination, samples were analyzed by gas chromatography (GC). The same reaction was performed using 10 mg of commercial TiO2 (P-25, Aldrich) to compare the catalytic activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic stability and self-oxidation tests for PE-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability of the PE-CQDs was examined during the Rhodamine B (RhB) photo-degradation reaction under a 400 W Xe-lamp (attached with an water-IR filter) illumination. We have carried out the degradation reaction with PE-CQDs up to the 10th cycle by repeatedly adding the dye solution without intermittent cleaning of the photocatalyst. After completion of the 10th cycle, the same PE-CQD solution was kept under the Xe-lamp for 10 h without any further addition of RhB when it was observed that the color of the solution (arising from PE-CQDs as there were no dye molecules left) becomes increasingly faint. On the other hand, two 50 mL glass beakers containing 10 mg PE-CQDs (10 mL water) in each were added separately and one of them was kept on continuous stirring under direct sunlight for 30 days while the other beaker was kept in dark for the same time. The solution was filtered and then the water content was allowed to evaporate naturally by keeping it open at room temperature.

The products of the self-sensitized photo-oxidation of PE-CQDs were investigated in a closed 50 mL RBF containing PE-CQDs aqueous solution with constant magnetic stirring at 300 rpm under different atmospheres. The PE-CQD concentration was kept same as O2 adsorption-desorption. A 400 W Xe-lamp attached with a water-IR filter was used as light source. The gaseous product so formed with regular time interval was analyzed through gas-chromatography (Shimadzu GC-2014).

The PE-CQDs were prepared using various commercially available high-density polyethylene (HDPE, such as a kitchen-glove, Fig. 1 a and b) and also confirmed by using research-grade HDPE. The small precursor polyethylene pieces turned into a charred, black-colored carbon-mass after treatment with 18 M H2SO4 under reflux conditions for 1 h due to removal of hydrogen from the polymer chain and its subsequent cyclization to form graphitic entities [9]. H2O2 was added drop-wise to this reaction aliquot for further oxidation, and the black-mass started disappearing almost immediately until a transparent, yellowish PE-CQD solution was obtained in about 2 min (See the schematic diagram in Fig. S1,†). It may be pointed out that in our earlier synthesis, KMnO4 and H2O2 were used as oxidizing agents by taking inspiration from the Hummer's method of graphene synthesis. Based on our current findings, it now occurs that the use of toxic KMnO4 is avoidable and H2O2 alone is capable of producing the CQDs. Also, we demonstrated earlier that this process produces gram quantities of PE-CQDs per hour of synthesis time. However, the left-over H2SO4 contains MnO2 causing difficulties in its subsequent use. With the use of H2O2 as oxidizing agent now, all solvents used in PE-CQD synthesis can be reused. In addition, since the synthesis process is residue-free and the PE-CQDs are monodisperse, these can be recovered fairly easily by simple evaporation of the extracting solvent, thus obliterating the need for time consuming chromatographic and dialysis techniques for purification.

The purity and crystallinity of the PE-CQDs were confirmed from the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD, Fig. S2,†) analysis which shows the presence of broad (002) graphitic planes, but no other impurity. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1c and d) revealed that the particles are spherical, measuring 1.5–7 nm in size and an average size of 4 nm as shown in Fig. 1e. Their size is somewhat smaller than the PE-CQDs synthesized using KMnO4 (average size of 6.5 nm). The presence of the graphitic regions can be clearly observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1f) having lattice-fringes separated by 0.32 nm corresponding to the (002) planes. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) shows the presence of sulphur in the sample (Fig. 1g) due to the functionalization of the PE-CQDs by sulphuric acid [9]. The nature of the various functional groups were examined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2 a, revealing the presence of several functional groups. The peak at 2900 cm−1 can be assigned to the C–H stretching vibration while the peaks at 1175 and 978 cm−1arise due to the S–O stretching vibrations of –SO3H. The sharp peaks at 1254, 1445, and 1723 cm−1 correspond to the C–O, C–O–C, and CO stretching vibrations, respectively, suggesting the formation of carbonyl and ether functional groups in PE-CQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis further confirmed the functional groups present on the PE-CQDs. Fig. 2b shows the core-level XPS spectrum of C 1s that could be fitted with 5 peaks. The peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.6 eV, 286.5 eV, 287.8 eV and 288.9 eV have been assigned to the sp
2 C–C, sp
3 C–C, C–O– (hydroxyl) and C–O–C (epoxy) groups, CO (carbonyl) species COOH (carboxyl) groups respectively with a relative abundance of 31%, 19%, 25%, 13% and 11%. The amount of oxygen forming single and double bonds were also estimated by deconvoluting the O 1s peak as shown in Fig. 2c. Since the peaks at 533.0 eV and 531.8 eV arise from the single-bonded and double-bonded oxygen species respectively,9 they have an atomic ratio of ∼40:60. A comparison with the KMnO4 generated PE-CQDs reveals that the present CQDs have less number of sp
2 carbon (31% vs. 49%), but more number of C-atoms as parts of hydroxyl or epoxy groups (25% vs. 11%), suggesting a higher extent of oxidation by H2O2 and a possibility to control the extent of functionalization by adjusting the oxidation step.

The aqueous solution of PE-CQDs exhibited novel optical properties as revealed by the UV–Visible (UV–Vis) absorption, photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, and time-correlated single-photon count (TCSPC) measurements. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibited an absorption onset at ∼550 nm (Fig. 2d) and other prominent absorption features at 340, 375, 395, and 425 nm. A typical PL spectrum of the solution is plotted in inset of Fig. 2d. We also recorded the PL spectra with different excitation-wavelengths (λex∼335–455 nm range) and observed that the emission peak wavelength (λem) is red-shifted with longer λex (Fig. 2e). The most intense emission maximum was observed at 510 nm and λex = 395 nm, corresponding to an emission quantum yield (QY) of ∼6.9%. In comparison, the PE-CQDs prepared with KMnO4 exhibited an emission maximum at 523 nm with a QY of 9.5%.

The shifts observed in the λem with changing λex are well-documented for many different CQDs [33]. However, the luminescence-decay measurements at various λem unrevealed an interesting pattern. Fig. 3 a is the luminescence decay plot for the PE-CQDs at 510 nm (λex = 375) corresponding to an average lifetime (τ) of 1.82 ns. Further, the sample was excited at 375 nm and the emission decay kinetics data were recorded at regular λem intervals in the 400–600 nm range. The data were fitted to a tri-exponential function and the typical fitting parameters are given in Table S1,†. The average lifetime (τ), was calculated using the following formula:
A v e r a g e
l i f e t i m e
( τ )
=
(
τ 1 A 1
+
τ 2 A 2
+
τ 3 A 3
)
/
(
A 1 + A 2 + A 3
)
where, τ1, τ2, and τ3are emission lifetimes in three different time stages and A1, A2, and A3are the amplitude of the different emission lifetimes. τ becomes maximum near the λem, max, but reduces at lower or higher λem (Fig. 3b). The minimum value of τ = 0.62 ns was recorded at λem = 400 nm, which gradually increases to 1.82 ns until λem = 510 nm or 520 nm and then goes on decreasing to 1.43 ns at λem = 600. To underline the differences in τ, the decay traces for the same decay counts are plotted at different λem within the first few nanoseconds where the fast decay component is clearly observed (Fig. 3b1-b6). Recently, we serendipitously observed that in the case of a light-harvesting semiconductor (g-C3N4), τ is λem dependent and gradually increases over ten times within the emission peak as λem becomes longer, thereby remarkably influencing the exciton-transfer dynamics in the event of photocatalytic reactions [34]. The effect was attributed to a vibrational relaxation process from the higher excited state to the band-edge. Surprisingly though, the behaviour of the PE-CQDs is quite different and probably complex in nature since the origin and the nature of the emission centers in CQDs are highly debatable [35–37].

Recently, it was shown that the PE-CQDs have the unique ability to capture oxygen molecules from the air and enrich the oxygen content of a solution. The molecular oxygen content per gram of the PE-CQDs is higher than those expected to be exhibited by the custom-made materials for gas adsorption such as zeolites and metal-organic frameworks [9,37]. We evaluated the oxygen enrichment properties of the H2O2 generated PE-CQDs by measuring the total O2 content (TO) of an aqueous solution containing 0.83 mg PE-CQDs per mL of water. The TO value of the solution was ∼652 ± 8 μM, more than double of that in pure water (∼340 μM, Fig. 4 a and b). Notably, the TO value is higher than the same exhibited by PE-CQDs prepared by using KMnO4 (640 ± 8 μM) [9]. Furthermore, it was argued in the previous study that the O2 molecules are loosely bound to the CQD surface and therefore the O2 level can be easily adjusted by replacing it with other gas molecules under high partial pressure. Thus, we studied the kinetics of O2 desorption under a continuous purging of N2 gas in the solution as shown in Fig. 4a and b. The TO level decreases rapidly to 125 μM within the first 3 min and attained a saturated depletion value of ∼90 μM in ∼5–6 min. In contrast, oxygen desorption from pure water is slower (Fig. 4a), requiring ∼20 min to reach a saturated depletion value of 5 μM.

The deoxygenated solution captures back the oxygen molecules from the air to attain the equilibrium value once the N2 flow is stopped. The O2 uptake features in the N2 purged pure water and aqueous PE-CQD solution in open air are also shown in Fig. 4a and b, suggesting a much faster rate of O2 uptake (∼3–4 times in the initial 2–3 min) in the case of the PE-CQD solution. Thus, by acting as carriers of O2 molecules from the air-water interface to the bulk of the solution, in addition to the typical diffusion of the gaseous O2 in the solution, the PE-CQDs are capable of inducing a markedly faster rate of O2 diffusion. The O2 uptake measurements were carried out at two different depths (0.2 cm and 8 cm) from the exposed air-aqueous interface to confirm the same. As expected (Fig. 4c), the time taken by the solution to attain oxygen saturation is proportional to the depth, while the saturation levels remain the same. On the other hand, by considering the increase in the TO content per minute, the O2 diffused rate is much smaller in pure water. We have attributed this high diffusion rate in the PE-CQD solution to a persistently high O2 concentration gradient across the air-water interface since the dissolved oxygen quickly associates with the PE-CQDs depleting the O2 levels in the surrounding [9,38].

Finally, we show that (i) the oxygen enrichment properties of the PE-CQDs in the solution remain reversible, as shown in five successive N2 purging cycles in Fig. 4d, and (ii) the enrichment is PE-CQDs concentration-dependent. Thus, the TO content of the solutions containing 5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg, 20 mg, 25 mg and 30 mg per 30 mL of water were 473, 522, 572, 611, 652, and 712 μM respectively (Fig. 4e). Overall, the PE-CQDs obtained using H2O2are similar to those prepared by using KMnO4 while exhibits an improved oxygen adsorption behaviour. The higher oxygen content in PE-CQDs generated with H2O2 is ascribed to the variation in functional groups from those prepared with KMnO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic oxidation of Benzyl alcohol by PE-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Selective photocatalytic oxidation of alcohols to the corresponding carbonyl compounds using molecular O2 as an oxidant has gained significant recent interest due to the greener synthetic approach [39–41]. The high visible light response and the copious O2content on the PE-CQD surface make them an ideal catalyst for the selective and controlled photo-oxidation of benzyl alcohol (BA) and its derivatives using molecular O2from air and sunlight. BA oxidation was carried out in acetonitrile solvent in an open-air under direct sunlight and the conversion efficiency was found to be 75% with >90% selectivity after 4 h of illumination. To confirm the role of molecular O2 in the reaction, we have performed the reaction by varying the O2 content in the reaction aliquot. Table 1 summarizes the result of the various oxidation reactions. Interestingly, we noted that the reaction yield barely increases when we purged O2 gas in the reaction vessel during oxidation, unlike other photocatalysts reported so far, suggesting that the PE-CQDs are sufficiently saturated with O2 molecules (Table 1, entry 2) [42–44]. To confirm this, two more sets of reactions were carried out by purging N2 through the reaction mixture before starting the illumination. In the first set, we have purged N2 through the reaction aliquot and then added PE-CQDs (which was kept in the air) to it, resulting in 54% conversion after 4 h (Table 1, entry 3a). In the second set, N2 was passed through the solution containing PE-CQDs to remove maximum possible dissolved O2 from the reaction mixture as well as the PE-CQD surface; where the resultant conversion efficiency was still quite high, though dropped to ∼34% after 4 h (Table 1, entry 4b). We anticipated that between the first and the second set of reactions, 1stshould give a better yield as we did not remove the O2 adsorbed on PE-CQDs surface, leading to a higher O2 content in the medium. In contrast, when oxygen was removed from the solvent as well as from the PE-CQDs surface in the 2nd set, the reaction efficiency decreased further by 20%. The findings are in stark contrast to the earlier reports, where the conversion becomes negligible under an inert atmosphere due to the lack of O2 in the reaction aliquot. A ∼54% conversion, even in an inert atmosphere, was observed for the first time because of the presence of O2 molecule on PE-CQDs surface, earmarking the influence of their oxygen uptake ability in oxidation reactions.

The conversion is negligible in the presence of PE-CQDs at room temperature under dark conditions inferring its photocatalytic nature. We then carried out the reaction by increasing the reaction temperatures to 40 °C and 50 °C under the dark conditions, but the yield did not improve (Table 1, entry 6&7). The conversion was negligible when the reaction was performed under sunlight in the open air in absence of PE-CQDs (Table 1, entry 8). Additionally, we have performed the same reaction using a 400 W Xe lamp (attached to an IR filter) and the conversion efficiency was found to be nearly identical to that of the reaction under direct sunlight (Table 1, entry 9).

The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for the photocatalytic benzyl alcohol oxidation to benzaldehyde was calculated using the following expression [45]:

The estimated AQE under direct sunlight was 8.36% for the BA oxidation reaction using the PE-CQDs at ambient conditions (Details in Note S1, Fig. S3,†).

The efficiency of the reaction was compared by using commercial TiO2–P25 as a photocatalyst and the conversion was ∼23% under identical conditions (Table 1, entry 10). The BA oxidation efficiency of the PE-CQDs higher in comparison to the other recently developed photocatalysts and also the PE-CQDs previously prepared using toxic KMnO4. It is to be pointed out that such oxidation reactions are often carried out at elevated temperatures and high oxygen pressure using other photocatalysts to improve the reaction kinetics [42,43,46–49]. In comparison, PE-CQDs exhibited a superior performance despite using ambient conditions and air as the source of O2. A performance comparison with other recently developed catalysts is provided in Table S2,†.

The versatility of the PE-CQDs was studied by performing the reaction with various electron-donating (-CH3, –OCH3, t-butyl) and electron-withdrawing (-F, -Cl, –NO2) group-substituted BA derivatives, as shown in Table 2 . The reaction-rate is almost similar with the electron-donating substituent groups while slightly slower in the case of BA molecules with electron-withdrawing groups. However, the reaction did not proceed even after 8 h of light irradiation when aliphatic alcohol was used, suggesting a high efficiency and selectivity of the PE-CQDs for the oxidation of various substituted aromatic alcohols.

Several controlled reactions were performed to clarify the mechanism of BA photo-oxidation and to find out the active intermediates. Molecular O2 as the oxidizing agent was confirmed by changing the reaction environment to N2 as discussed earlier. Upon light-illumination on PE-CQDs, the photogenerated electrons and holes interact with O2 and BA. The scavengers used to find out the reaction mechanism are electron scavenger (K2S2O8), superoxide radical anion (benzoquinone), hole scavenger (ammonium oxalate), and carbon radical scavenger (butylated-hydroxyl toluene). The addition K2S2O8 to the reaction-mixture induces a sharp decrease in the conversion efficiency to ∼23% (Fig. S4a,†). We have also observed a decrease in efficiency by adding benzoquinone to the reaction (Fig. S4a, b,†). When we performed the reaction in the presence of ammonium oxalate, the conversion decreased drastically to 8%, indicating the major influence of holes during oxidation (Fig. 4a,†). Further, we have performed a reaction in the presence of butylatedhydroxytoluene as a carbon-radical scavenger and the conversion was suppressed to ∼7% after 4 h suggesting that oxidation reaction indeed goes via formation of carbon radical species (Fig. 4a,†).

Based on these observations, we propose a reaction mechanism as shown in Scheme S1,†. Briefly, an adsorbed O2 molecule takes one photogenerated electron and forms a superoxide radical anion, O2˙− (a). Next, O2˙− extracts a proton from a neighbouring BA molecule and a photogenerated hole, producing an oxygen radical species (c). Further, (c) converts toa carbon radical species (d) with the help of another exciton and the adsorbed superoxide radical leaves in the form of H2O2. The in-situ generation of ‘d’ was confirmed by using a trapping reagent, TEMPO ((2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-yl)oxyl radical) and detecting with high-resolution mass spectroscopy (Fig. S5,†) [50]. The formation of H2O2 in the reaction aliquot was confirmed colorimetrically by KMnO4 titration (Fig. S6,†) [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Of stability, light-induced oxidative elimination or autophagy of the PE-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the stability of the PE-CQDs, we have performed the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine-B (RhB) dye as model-reaction up to the 10th catalytic cycle and followed it by recording temporal UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S7, S8,†).98% of degradation was observed in 14 min of light-illumination in the first cycle. It has become marginally slower with every cycle, though the degradation was >94% in 14 min at the end of the 10th cycle. The corresponding rate-constants observed in the consecutive cycles are given in Fig. 5 a, showing a minimal loss of efficiency. Notably, the RhB degradation follows the de-ethylation mechanism as confirmed by the formation of a new UV–Vis absorption peak at ∼504 nm, which is attributed to the formation of the Rhodamine 110 as an intermediate in the process and confirmed by using mass spectrometry [9]. We point out that the degradation efficiency exhibited by the PE-CQDs is extremely high, depicting at least 5-10-fold improvement over any state-of-the-art catalyst known to us, including the noble-metal based materials (see Table S3,†). The PE-CQDs after catalysis (which took 2.5 h) were recovered and analyzed using TEM, UV–visible absorption, and PL measurements (Fig. S9,†) to show that they remain unaffected by the catalysis process.

The PE-CQDs under illumination, however, exhibited a rather unexpected behaviour in the absence of the reactant molecules. Even though their stability is high during photocatalysis (earlier we demonstrated that the efficiency is quite remarkable even up to 100 cycles using the KMnO4 generated PE-CQDs) [9], when we continued to irradiate the same PE-CQD solution at the end of the 10th cycle for a prolonged period (10 h) without further RhB addition, a visible and gradual change in the solution color was noticed, as perceived from the photographs shown in Fig. 5b (b1-b3). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the solution (Fi hat the absorption intensities decrease steadily with irradiation time indicating the loss of light absorption sites, or probably the PE-CQDs themselves. The intensity of the PE-CQD absorption peak decreases during photocatalysis too, but slowly as indicated by the inflection point in the inset of Fig. 5b.

Photobleaching on carbon-dots has been widely observed wherein the changes in their color (and luminescence intensity) are irreversible and occur at different rates in the presence or the absence of oxygen [19]. Partial removal or changes in the structure of the fluorophore functional groups have been proposed as the origin of photobleaching, accompanied by an increase in –C-O functionalities indicating that a photo-oxidation process takes part in it [20,22]. However, the extent of such an effect and if the same can be utilized usefully is not clear yet. To understand the origin of color change in our experiments, we illuminated an aqueous PE-CQD solution with the same Xe lamp in a closed-vessel and measured the variations in the TO content with illumination time. The TO content was nearly constant for the first 90 min and then started decreasing from 650 μM to 230 μM at a rate of ∼40 μM/h, as shown in Fig. 6 a, indicating that O2 ought to be consumed in some process in the presence of light since it cannot escape the vessel. Therefore, the air inside the vessel was analyzed before and after light-illumination. The gas chromatography analysis of the air inside the vessel confirmed the formation of carbon dioxide inside the vessel (Fig. 6a inset). The CO2 quantity steadily increases with irradiation time (Fig. 6b) implying that the PE-CQDs self-oxidize to form CO2 in the solution. To examine if the CO2 formation is a consequence of aerial oxidation or at the expense of the oxygenated functional groups, we have further carried out the same experiment but first purging the PE-CQD solution thoroughly with N2. No appreciable amount for CO2was found to be forming in that case even after 12 h (Fig. 6b) suggesting that the photo-oxidation of the PE-CQDs occurs using the dissolved or surface-adsorbed O2 molecules (Fig. 6c). It further implies that their photobleaching can be initiated or arrested by using an appropriate atmosphere. Thus, the PE-CQD photo-oxidation accelerates once the desired reaction is over, while on the other hand, it remains more-or-less intact while catalyzing it.

A probable reason for not detecting the CO2 formation by others so far is that the formation is a rather slow process and also, we could observe sufficient CO2only when using a larger quantity of CQDs. At the same time, the larger quantity makes it difficult to notice minute color-changes. Therefore, we investigated as to what extent the photo-oxidation continues and does it stop at all. As shown in Fig. 7 , we took 10 mg PE-CQD in 10 mL water in an open beaker and monitored it in natural sunlight for 38 days. The color slowly faded with each passing day and became nearly colorless on the 38th day. No color change was observed in a similar beaker kept in dark. The water-content was then allowed to be evaporated naturally, upon which it was observed that the PE-CQDs disappeared from the beaker exposed to sunlight, while those in the beaker kept in dark could be fully recovered. Therefore, we conclude that the PE-CQDs got oxidized to CO2.

Uncommon photodegradation pathways of self-sensitized photo-oxidation of organic pollutants in aqueous solution have been observed in certain compounds [52–54]. Therein, degradation is mediated by the in-situ generated reactive species such as singlet oxygen, superoxide anion radical, hydroxyl radical, etc. We too have demonstrated that the O2 molecules adsorbed on PE-CQDs form superoxide radicals in presence of light, and this radical is responsible for the BA photo-oxidation reactions, as discussed above. Therefore, given that the PE-CQDs are more stable in the presence of reactants, it seems that the superoxide radicals exert a choice between the reactant molecules or the carbon atoms on the PE-CQDs to react with and when left with no choice, it rapidly oxidizes the PE-CQDs to CO2. The self-sensitized photo-oxidation of CQDs is unique among nanomaterials as it does not leave behind any residue. No other nanomaterial is known to do so (or even organic molecules that completely convert to CO2), to the best of our knowledge. Considering that a PE-CQD eliminates itself rapidly from the system after completing its task, we propose that the process is termed as ‘carbon-dot autophagy (CDA)’ (meaning ‘self-devouring’ or eating its own parts hungrily). CDA may be very useful because if the CQDs are exposed to or left unattended in the atmosphere, they will disappear with time and merge in air.

In summary, we have been able to convert waste polyethylene completely to carbon quantum dots in a greener way by a simple acid-mediated heat treatment method. The reagents used for this conversion may either be recovered back or leave behind no residue. The PE-CQDs enrich its solution with molecular oxygen harvested from the air with more than 1% of its weight under ambient conditions (and also induces a faster oxygen diffusion rate) which is higher than the same expected from conventional materials such as zeolites, etc. The number of surface functional groups on PE-CQDs can be controlled by tuning the oxidation step of the carbon material derived from polyethylene to realize improved oxygen enrichment properties and photocatalytic oxidation efficiency. This was demonstrated using photo-oxidation of benzyl alcohols using ambient conditions, where the oxidation rate is higher than most state-of-the-art photocatalysts despite using the milder conditions. Similarly, the rate of pollutant dye degradation is at least five times better than other photocatalysts reported to date. Importantly, we observed that in the absence of the reactants, the PE-CQDs undergo self-sensitized photo-oxidation forming CO2 and leading to its complete removal from the system. The extinction process that is unlike any other photocatalyst known to date, maybe termed as ‘carbon-dot autophagy’ and is deemed advantageous as no separation process for the PE-CQDs is required. Particularly so because the separation of carbon-dots from a reaction mixture is tedious as they are highly soluble and also when they may be used for waste-water treatment from where recovery is not desired.

Sanjit Mondal: Conceptualization, Methodology. C.P. Vinod: Surface characterization, Writing – review & editing. Ujjal K. Gautam: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
